passages.title: MAMMOTH KILL
passages.source_html:
MAMMOTH KILL


What Led to the disappearance of the giant mammals? Kate Wong examines the theories.


A


Although it's hard to imagine in this age of urban sprawl and automobiles, North America once belonged to huge, elephant-like mammoths, camels, bear-sized beavers and other giant beasts, collectively known as ‘megafauna’. Some 11,000 years ago, however, these large-bodied mammals – about 70 species in all – disappeared. Their demise coincided roughly with the arrival of humans in this region and dramatic climate change – factors that have inspired several theories about the die off. Yet despite decades of scientific investigation, the exact cause remains a mystery. Now new findings offer support to one of these controversial hypotheses: that human hunting drove these huge megafauna species to extinction.


B


This belief resulted in the overkill model which emerged in the 1960s, when it was put forth by Paul S Martin of the University of Arizona. Since then, critics have charged that no archaeological remains exist to support the idea that the first Americans hunted to the extent necessary to cause these extinctions. But at the annual meeting of the Society of Vertebrate Paleontology in Mexico City in October 1999, specialist John Alroy of the University of California at Santa Barbara argued that in fact, hunting-driven extinction is not only plausible, it was unavoidable. He has determined, using a computer simulation, that even a very modest amount of hunting would have wiped out these animals.


C


Assuming an initial human population of 100 people that grew no more than two percent annually, Alroy determined that, if each band of, say, 50 people killed 15 to 20 large animals a year, humans could have eliminated the animal populations within 1,000 years. Large mammals in particular would have been vulnerable to the pressure because they have longer gestation periods than smaller mammals and their young require extended care.


D


However, not everyone agrees with Alroy's assessment. For one thing, the results depend on population size estimates for the extinct animals – estimates that are not necessarily reliable. But a more specific criticism comes from mammal expert Ross DE Macphee of the American Museum of Natural History in New York City, who points out that the relevant archaeological record contains barely a dozen examples of stone points embedded in mammoth bones (and none, it should be noted, are known from other megafaunal remains) – hardly what one might expect if hunting drove these animals to extinction. Furthermore, some of these species had a vast range, covering the whole continent – the Jefferson's Ground Sloth, for example, lived as far north as the Yukon and as far south as Mexico – which would have made hunting them in numbers sufficient to cause their extinction rather unlikely, he says.


E


Macphee agrees that humans most likely brought about these extinctions (as well as others around the world that coincided with human arrival), but not directly. Rather than through hunting, he suggests that people may have introduced a deadly disease. Perhaps through their dogs or accompanying vermin, which then spread wildly among the native species because of their low resistance to the new introductions. Repeated outbreaks of a deadly disease could thus quickly drive them to the point of no return. So far, Macphee does not have empirical evidence for this theory, and it will not be easy to come by: such disease would kill far too quickly to leave its signature on the bones themselves. But he hopes that analyses of tissue and DNA from the most recent animal remains will eventually reveal the microbes responsible.


F


The third explanation for what brought on this North American extinction does not involve human beings. Instead, its proponents blame the loss on the climate. The Pleistocene epoch in question witnessed considerable climate instability, explains Russell W Graham of the Denver Museum of Nature and Science. As a result, their regular habitats disappeared, and species that had once formed communities split apart. For some animals, this brought opportunity. For much of the megafauna, however, the increasingly uniform terrain left them with shrinking geographical ranges – a death sentence for large animals, which need correspondingly large ranges. Although these creatures managed to maintain viable populations through most of the Pleistocene Period, the final major climate fluctuation pushed them over the edge, Graham says.


G


For his part, Alroy is still convinced that human hunters were the destroyers of the giant animals. The igo overkill model explains everything the disease and climate scenarios explain, he asserts, and in addition makes accurate predictions about which species would eventually become extinct.

groups.title: Questions 14-26
groups.source_html:
Questions 14 - 20


Complete the notes. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS in each gap.

14

Three theories have been put forward to explain the disappearance of the different species of large mammals that inhabited 11,000 years ago. The proposed around fifty years ago by Paul S Martin, blames by people for mass extinction. Computer calculations seem to support this explanation, but critics question the reliability of the figures they are based on.


The second theory suggests that humans introduced a which wipe out the large mammals. However, so far this theory also lacks any .


The final theory suggests that this period experienced significant which eventually led to the loss of habitat and to the division of the that some of the large mammals had organised.

正确答案： 14 North America  |  15 overkill model  |  16 hunting  |  17 disease##deadly disease  |  18 empirical evidence  |  19 climate instability  |  20 communities
Question 14

定位信息：

11,000 years ago


对应原文：

A段：“Although it's hard to imagine in this age of urban sprawl and automobiles, North America once belonged to huge, elephant-like mammoths, camels, bear-sized beavers and other giant beasts, collectively known as ‘megafauna’. Some 11,000 years ago, however, these large-bodied mammals – about 70 species in all – disappeared.”


解题思路：

原文提到11,000年前，北美洲曾经有巨型哺乳动物，因此答案是“North America”。题目中的“large mammals”在原文中对应“giant beasts”。

Question 15

定位信息：

fifty years ago, Paul S Martin


对应原文：

B段：“This belief resulted in the overkill model which emerged in the 1960s, when it was put forth by Paul S Martin of the University of Arizona.”


解题思路：

原文提到“overkill model”是由Paul S Martin在20世纪60年代提出的，因此答案是“overkill model”。题目中的“proposed”在原文中对应“emerged”。

Question 16

答案：hunting


定位信息：

blames, mass extinction


对应原文：

A段：“Now new findings offer support to one of these controversial hypotheses: that human hunting drove these huge megafauna species to extinction.”


解题思路：

原文提到该理论认为人类狩猎导致了巨型哺乳动物的灭绝，因此答案是“hunting”。

Question 17

定位信息：

second theory, introduce


对应原文：

E段：“…, he suggests that people may have introduced a deadly disease. Perhaps through their dogs or accompanying vermin, which then spread wildly among the native species…”


解题思路：

原文提到第二种理论认为人类引入了一种致命的疾病，导致大型哺乳动物灭绝，因此答案是“disease”。题目中的“wipe out”在原文中对应“deadly”。

Question 18

定位信息：

theory, lacks


对应原文：

E段：“So far, Macphee does not have empirical evidence for this theory…”


解题思路：

原文明确提到目前没有经验证据支持这种理论，因此答案是“empirical evidence”。题目中的“lacks”在原文中对应“does not have”。

Question 19

定位信息：

period, significant


对应原文：

F段：“The third explanation for what brought on this North American extinction does not involve human beings. Instead, its proponents blame the loss on the climate. The Pleistocene epoch in question witnessed considerable climate instability,...”


解题思路：

原文提到该时期经历了显著的气候不稳定，因此答案是“climate instability”。题目中的“significant”在原文中对应“considerable”。

Question 20

定位信息：

loss of habitat, division


对应原文：

F段：“As a result, their regular habitats disappeared, and species that had once formed communities split apart.”


解题思路：

原文提到物种形成的社区分裂，因此答案是“communities”。题目中的“division”在原文中对应“split apart”。

Questions 21 - 26


Choose the correct person (A - C) for each statement. You may choose any correct person more than once.


List of People

A John Alroy

B Ross DE Macphee

C Russell W Graham

Question / ParagraphABCDEFG
21

21 Too little evidence exists to support the hunting theory.
✅ 正确答案：B

定位信息：

little evidence, hunting theory


对应原文：

D段：“But a more specific criticism comes from mammal expert Ross DE Macphee of the American Museum of Natural History in New York City, who points out that the relevant archaeological record contains barely a dozen examples of stone points embedded in mammoth bones (and none, it should be noted, are known from other megafaunal remains) – hardly what one might expect if hunting drove these animals to extinction.”


解题思路：

原文中Macphee指出几乎没有考古证据支持狩猎理论，因此答案是Ross DE Macphee。题目中的“little evidence”在原文中对应“barely a dozen examples”。
22

22 The bigger the animal, the bigger the territory it requires for survival.
✅ 正确答案：C

定位信息：

Russell W Graham


对应原文：

F段：“For much of the megafauna, however, the increasingly uniform terrain left them with shrinking geographical ranges – a death sentence for large animals, which need correspondingly large ranges.Although these creatures managed to maintain viable populations through most of the Pleistocene Period, the final major climate fluctuation pushed them over the edge, Graham says.”


解题思路：

原文中Graham提到大动物需要相应大的栖息地，因此答案是C（Russell W Graham）。题目中的“bigger the animal, the bigger the territory”在原文中对应“large animals…need correspondingly large ranges”。
23

23 Globally, humans have been indirectly responsible for the elimination of many species.
✅ 正确答案：B

定位信息：

Ross DE Macphee


对应原文：

E段：“Macphee agrees that humans most likely brought about these extinctions…but not directly.”


解题思路：

原文中Macphee认为人类可能间接导致了这些灭绝，因此答案是B（Ross DE Macphee）。题目中的“indirectly responsible”在原文中对应“not directly”。
24

24 Population estimates can be used to understand how large mammals become extinct
✅ 正确答案：A

对应原文：

C段：“Assuming an initial human population of 100 people that grew no more than two percent annually, Alroy determined that, if each band of, say, 50 people killed 15 to 20 large animals a year, humans could have eliminated the animal populations within 1,000 years.”


解题思路：

原文中Alroy使用人口估计来理解大型哺乳动物的灭绝过程，因此答案是A（John Alroy）。题目中的“Population estimates”在原文中对应“Alroy determined…”.
25

25 Scientific examination of fossil remains may provide some proof for one of the theories.
✅ 正确答案：B

定位信息：

Scientific examination, fossil remains


对应原文：

E段：“But he hopes that analyses of tissue and DNA from the most recent animal remains will eventually reveal the microbes responsible.”


解题思路：

原文中Macphee希望通过科学分析化石遗骸来证明他的理论，因此答案是B（Ross DE Macphee）。题目中的“Scientific examination…provide some proof”在原文中对应“analyses…will eventually reveal”。
26

26 Environmental changes negatively affected the social groupings of some large species.
✅ 正确答案：C

定位信息：

Russell W Graham


对应原文：

F段：“The Pleistocene epoch in question witnessed considerable climate instability, explains Russell W Graham of the Denver Museum of Nature and Science. As a result, their regular habitats disappeared, and species that had once formed communities split apart.”


解题思路：

原文中Graham提到环境变化导致物种形成的社区分裂，因此答案是C（Russell W Graham）。题目中的“Environmental changes”在原文中对应“climate instability”和“regular habitats disappeared”。
